food_name: One Duck and Three Meals (Whole Roast Duck Set)
aliases: Whole Duck with Duck Soup and Salt Pepper Duck Rack

english: Whole Duck with Duck Soup and Salt Pepper Duck Rack

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a multi-course roast duck set meal. FOODON_03301116 (duck food product) is the closest parent category covering duck-based preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers duck meat as a category. No specific code exists for a composite roast duck set meal with soup and rack components. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for a composite roast duck set meal. SNOMED CT does not provide sufficient granularity for this multi-course dish.

serving.description: 1 full set (whole roast duck + duck soup + salt and pepper duck rack) shared by 4–6 persons
serving.natural_language_portion: A full set comprising a whole roast duck (approximately 800 g edible yield), a bowl of duck carcass soup, and a portion of salt and pepper duck rack, typically shared among 4 to 6 diners at a Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05140); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for duck soup and fried duck components
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: This is a composite three-component set meal. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated as a weighted blend of: (1) roast duck meat and skin (USDA SR Legacy NDB 05140, ~55% weight share), (2) duck soup with minimal solids (USDA FoodData Central duck broth analogue, ~20% weight share), and (3) salt and pepper fried duck rack (USDA SR Legacy roast duck with added sodium adjustment for frying and seasoning, ~25% weight share). The blended per-100 g values reflect the edible composite. Sodium is elevated due to seasoning and soup components. Values are estimates; a direct HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this specific dish was not found.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous meat-based dish entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a roast duck set meal. GI is estimated at 25 based on analogous high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate meat dishes (roast meats, poultry) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (1.2 g per 100 g), placing it firmly in the low-GI category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 14.4) / 100 = 3.6, where serving carbohydrate is estimated at 14.4 g for a 1200 g set shared across 4–6 persons (200 g individual portion × 1.2 g carb per 100 g × 6 = 14.4 g per person equivalent).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seasoning, soy sauce marinade, and duck soup broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium preparation, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g — driven by soy sauce, salt seasoning, and soup broth — can contribute substantially to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg when consumed in typical restaurant portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (approximately 1.2 g per 100 g) and a low estimated glycemic index of 25, making it a low glycemic load option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat and sodium content warrant attention to overall dietary balance and portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated cholesterol from duck skin and organ-adjacent meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Duck meat with skin contains moderate saturated fat (approximately 4.9 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (approximately 89 mg per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should consider removing the skin to reduce saturated fat intake and limit the frequency of consumption of this dish as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: One Duck and Three Meals (一鸭三吃, Yi Ya San Chi) is a traditional Chinese multi-course set meal built around a single whole duck, prepared and served in three distinct ways to maximise the use of the entire bird. The set typically comprises: (1) a whole roast duck with crispy lacquered skin, seasoned with five-spice powder, soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and rice wine, then roasted until the skin is golden and crackling; (2) a clear or lightly seasoned duck carcass soup made by simmering the duck bones with ginger, spring onion, and white pepper to produce a rich, savoury broth; and (3) a salt and pepper duck rack — the remaining frame and attached meat, deep-fried or wok-fried with salt, white pepper, garlic, and chilli for a crispy, aromatic finish. In Singapore, this dish is served at Chinese restaurants as a banquet or family-sharing centrepiece, priced as a premium set. The dish is nutritionally dense, providing high-quality protein (~19.8 g per 100 g), moderate to high fat (~17.2 g per 100 g including skin), and very low carbohydrate (~1.2 g per 100 g), resulting in a low glycemic index estimated at 25. The sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to soy-based marinades and soup seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is not halal-certified in most Singapore Chinese restaurants. It is dairy-free and gluten-free only if tamari is substituted for soy sauce. Clinically, it is suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns but should be consumed in moderation by those managing cardiovascular risk due to its saturated fat and cholesterol content from duck skin.

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific composite three-component duck set meal. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy roast duck data (NDB 05140) with weighted blending across the three sub-components. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database meat-dish analogues. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (roast duck) has strong USDA SR Legacy coverage and the composite estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. This is a composite set meal comprising three sub-dishes from one whole duck: whole roast duck, duck carcass soup, and salt and pepper duck rack. Nutrient values represent a weighted blend per 100 g of the edible composite. 2. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero as duck meat and its preparations contain negligible vitamin C, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). 3. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce marinade, salt seasoning, and soup broth; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. 4. The dish is served at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant (万家福创新中国菜) on Circular Road, Singapore, at SGD 112.80 for the full set. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 05140 roast duck values and are not independently verified for this specific restaurant preparation. 6. GI set to 25 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database meat-dish entries); gi_category is low (≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated at 3.6 for a 200 g individual portion equivalent. 7. FoodOn FOODON_03301116 and FoodEx2 A00LC are closest parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this composite set meal. 8. The dish is not halal-certified; pork is not an ingredient but cross-contamination risk exists in a Chinese restaurant kitchen.
